This stage is cross-cutting compile-time infrastructure: it does not participate in startup, the main loop, or teardown directly, but shapes how the rest of the codebase declares and enforces API contracts before the program runs. Its job is to turn source-level annotations into consistent generated code so experimental interfaces are tracked uniformly across the system.

The stage is currently centered on codex-experimental-api-macros/src/lib.rs, which defines the #[derive(ExperimentalApi)] procedural macro. When applied to structs or enums, this macro inspects annotated fields and variants and emits the supporting code needed to enforce experimental-API rules at runtime. That generated code performs checks when those members are used and also registers metadata into the project’s inventory so experimental surface area can be discovered centrally rather than by ad hoc manual bookkeeping.

In practice, this stage lets feature authors mark unstable API elements declaratively, while downstream runtime components consume the generated checks and registrations. The result is a single compile-time mechanism that keeps experimental API semantics consistent, discoverable, and hard to forget.
